mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the Warring States period (481-221 BCE), the breakdown of the Chou feudal system, the Hundred Schools of Thought including Confucianism, Daoism, Legalism, and Mohism, and the eventual unification under Qin, mapping to B.2.d and B.2.e.

Confucianism as founded by Confucius (551-479 BCE), its core teachings on benevolence and governance, and its adoption as state ideology under the Han dynasty directly correspond to B.2.e (Classical period philosophy) and C.2.c.i (Han political system).

Chinese Legalism's emphasis on strict laws and meritocratic bureaucracy under Shang Yang and Han Feizi, and its role in Qin unification and centralization, directly correspond to B.2.e (the Legalists) and C.1 (establishment of the Ch'in empire).

The Han Synthesis blending Confucian, Legalist, and Daoist thought, Dong Zhongshu's cosmological Confucianism, and the establishment of the imperial examination system directly correspond to C.2.c.i (the Han political system) and Han cultural developments.

Romance of the Three Kingdoms covers the historical period of the Han Dynasty's fall and the Three Kingdoms era (220-280 CE), directly corresponding to D.1.a (division of the empire into Wei, Shu Han, and Wu).

The Great Wall's construction from the Warring States through the Qin and Han dynasties, its defence against the Xiongnu, and its relationship to the Silk Road correspond to C.1.b (Shih Huang Ti's Great Wall) and C.2.c.ii (relations with the Hsiung-nu).

mappings.episodeTitle: Sun Tzu and The Art of War
mappings.rationaleAI: Sun Tzu and The Art of War from the 5th-century BCE Warring States period, covering military strategy and its relationship to Chinese philosophical thought, directly corresponds to B.2.e (the Classical period) and B.2.d.ii (military innovations during the Warring States).

Sources of early Chinese history including oracle bones, Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian, and bamboo strip texts directly correspond to outline A on historical sources and B.2.b on the Shang period's script and cultural artefacts.

The Battle of Talas (751) between the Abbasid Caliphate and the Tang Dynasty, halting Chinese westward expansion and facilitating technology transfer, corresponds to D.2.c.ii (T'ai Tsung's conquests and later military reverses under the early T'ang).
